1.17: A type: 'Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' Jesus himself identifies this as the OT's appointed sign for his resurrection: 'For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matt 12:40). The typological elements: Jonah goes into the deep as a substitute (the sailors are saved from the storm by throwing Jonah overboard; 1:12-15) → Jonah is entombed in the fish → Jonah emerges to complete his mission to the Gentiles. The antitype: Christ goes to the cross as the world's substitute → Christ is entombed → Christ rises and commissions the mission to all Gentiles (Matt 28:19). Jonah's Gentile mission to Nineveh is the shadow; the apostolic mission to all nations is the substance.
1.1: A theme: the prophetic commissioning that opens Jonah participates in the pattern of YHWH sending his word through a human messenger to a people who need to hear it. The entire prophetic tradition — every “the word of the LORD came” — points toward the Word who comes in person: “In many and various ways God spoke to our fathers through the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us through his Son” (Heb 1:1-2). Jonah is the last in a long line of those to whom the word came; Christ is the Word.
1.2: A revelation of God: YHWH's concern is not confined to Israel — it extends to Nineveh, the capital of the most powerful and most feared empire of the day, because “their wickedness has come up before me.” This reveals the God of universal moral sovereignty who cares about the conduct of all nations. The NT presents this character as expressed definitively in Christ: “For God so loved the world” (John 3:16) — the same God who was troubled by Nineveh's wickedness sent his Son not to condemn the world but to save it (John 3:17). The Great Commission (Matt 28:19) is the Jonah-commission fully obeyed.
1.3: A type by contrast: Jonah's flight from YHWH's presence is the anti-shape that Christ fills positively. Where Jonah runs to Tarshish (the farthest known point west), Christ “set his face to go to Jerusalem” (Luke 9:51) — the city of his death — and went willingly. Where Jonah “went down” (the verb yarad appears three times in v3) in a trajectory of descent from the call, Christ's descent was voluntary and purposeful: from heaven to manger to Galilee to Jerusalem to Gethsemane to the cross. The contrast clarifies what faithful obedience looks like — and underlines the costliness of what Christ did where Jonah did not.
1.4: A shadow: YHWH “hurled a great wind upon the sea” to pursue the fleeing prophet — the storm as the instrument of divine discipline. In the NT, Christ's disciples face a storm on the Sea of Galilee with Jesus asleep in the boat (Mark 4:37-38). The contrast is sharp: in Jonah, the storm is divine judgment directed at the guilty prophet; in Mark, the storm threatens the disciples traveling with the innocent one. Christ's authority over the wind and sea (“Peace! Be still!”) demonstrates that the God who commanded the storm in Jonah is the same God present in Christ — sovereign over all creaturely powers.
1.5: A shadow: Jonah is asleep below deck in the hold of the ship while the storm rages — the guilty prophet at rest while the innocent suffer. This is the negative form of Christ's sleep in the storm (Mark 4:38). Jonah's sleep is the sleep of one fleeing responsibility; Christ's sleep is the sleep of one whose identity as Lord of creation means he does not fear what the disciples fear. The captain's words to Jonah (“How can you sleep? Get up!”) are the obverse of the disciples' cry to Christ (“Teacher, do you not care that we are perishing?”); both scenes expose who the sleeping man really is.
1.6: A revelation of God: the captain's desperate plea — “Perhaps your god will give a thought to us, so that we do not perish” — is the voice of humanity in extremis, calling to any god who might hear. The Gentile captain does not know YHWH but gropes toward the possibility of divine mercy. Christ is the answer to this universal groping: “I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved” (John 10:9). The “perhaps” of the Gentile captain becomes the “whoever believes” of John 3:16 — the uncertainty of natural religious instinct replaced by the certainty of the word of Christ.
1.7: A theme: the casting of lots to identify the guilty one resonates with the NT's crucifixion scene, where lots are cast — but over Christ's clothing (Matt 27:35, fulfilling Ps 22:18), not to identify him. The contrast matters: lots identify Jonah as guilty; Christ's identity needed no lots because he was publicly condemned by accusation, mockery, and the judicial process. Jonah hides his guilt; Christ's innocence is publicly declared by Pilate (“I find no basis for a charge against this man”, Luke 23:4) even as he is condemned. What the process of identification reveals is different in each case.
1.8: A theme: Jonah's confession — “I am a Hebrew, and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land” — is theologically precise but situationally ironic; he fears the God he is fleeing. The irony illuminates a genuine spiritual condition: knowing the truth about God without living in conformity to it. Christ, by contrast, is the one in whom profession and practice are perfectly unified — he is the truth (John 14:6), not merely one who knows it. The gap between Jonah's confession and conduct is the gap that Christ's incarnation bridges: he is what Jonah claimed to be.
1.9: A theme: the sailors' “what have you done?” in response to Jonah's flight is the voice of moral perception — the Gentiles recognize the gravity of what has happened before Jonah acknowledges it. This inverts the expected hierarchy (Jew > Gentile in moral perception). Christ's parable of the Good Samaritan enacts the same inversion: the one who shows mercy is the despised outsider (Luke 10:33-37). The Gentiles who fear YHWH in this chapter prefigure the Gentiles who receive the gospel more readily than those to whom it first came (Matt 21:43).
1.10: A revelation of God: the sailors' fear when they hear that Jonah has fled from YHWH's presence — “What is this you have done?” — expresses reverence for the inescapability of YHWH's authority. The NT's form of this recognition is the disciples' “Who then is this, that even wind and sea obey him?” (Mark 4:41) and Peter's “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord” (Luke 5:8). Holiness that produces awe in those who encounter it is consistent across the testaments — and it reaches its fullest expression in the incarnate Son.
1.11: A theme: the sailors asking “what shall we do with you, that the sea may quiet down for us?” is the practical question of rescue: what action will bring peace to the chaos? This is, structurally, the question of salvation — what must be done to be saved? Jonah's answer (throw me into the sea) is the shadow of the gospel answer: the one who is given up for the many. But the contrast is maintained: Jonah is thrown because of his own guilt; Christ gave himself voluntarily for ours (Gal 2:20: “who loved me and gave himself for me”).
1.12: A type: “Pick me up and hurl me into the sea; then the sea will quiet down for you, for I know it is because of me that this great tempest has come upon you.” This is the book's most direct structural type of substitutionary atonement: one man identifies himself as the cause of the storm that threatens the many; one man is given up; the storm ceases; the many are saved. Christ is the antitype with the crucial difference: Jonah is guilty, Christ is innocent (2 Cor 5:21: “God made him who knew no sin to be sin for us”). The self-offering of the guilty one becomes in Christ the self-offering of the sinless one — the only substitution that can truly satisfy.
1.13: A theme: the sailors “rowed hard to get back to dry land, but they could not” — their effort to avoid the cost of the solution, to find another way. The NT equivalent is the disciples' inability to heal the demonized boy (Matt 17:16: “they could not heal him”) and Peter's doomed attempt to walk on water before faith falters (Matt 14:30). Human effort to avoid or achieve the solution to the human problem consistently fails. Only the action of YHWH — whether commanding the sea to calm or raising Christ from the dead — accomplishes what human effort cannot.
1.14: A revelation of God: the sailors' prayer before throwing Jonah overboard — “O LORD, let us not perish for this man's life, and lay not on us innocent blood” — is one of the most precise moral prayers in Scripture. They ask YHWH to absorb the guilt of their necessary action. This is structurally the prayer that Pilate's handwashing attempts to express (Matt 27:24: “I am innocent of this man's blood”) — but at the crucifixion it is YHWH himself who bears the “innocent blood” through the death of his Son, so that those who call on his name need not carry it (Acts 2:38).
1.15: A type: “So they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging.” Jonah enters the sea; the storm ceases; the sailors are saved. The typological structure is explicit in Jesus's identification (Matt 12:40), but the pattern itself is the logic of substitutionary atonement embedded in narrative: the handing over of one person into judgment results in peace for the many. At the cross, Christ is handed over (Rom 4:25: “who was delivered over to death for our sins”) into the sea of divine judgment — and the result is reconciliation, peace with God (Rom 5:1).
1.16: A shadow: the sailors “feared the LORD greatly, and they offered a sacrifice to the LORD and made vows.” Gentile sailors come to faith in YHWH through the crisis they experienced with Jonah — the one whose disobedience threatened their lives paradoxically becomes the instrument of their conversion. This anticipates the Gentile mission: through the death and resurrection of the one who “went down” (like Jonah), countless Gentiles come to worship YHWH. Cornelius's household (Acts 10), the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8), and the Greek God-fearers (Acts 17:4) are the fullness of what these sailors begin.

2.1: A type: “From inside the fish, Jonah prayed to the LORD his God.” Prayer from within the belly of the fish — prayer from what amounts to the grave — is the OT form of Christ's intercession from within death. Hebrews 5:7 says Christ “offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard.” Jonah in the fish praying and being heard (v10: YHWH commanded the fish) is the type of Christ in death praying and being heard in the resurrection. The prayer that rises from the deepest place is heard — this is the theological principle that ch. 2 demonstrates and the resurrection confirms.
2.2: A type: “From the depths of Sheol I cried for help, and you heard my voice.” Jonah describes his location as Sheol — the realm of the dead. His prayer from Sheol is the OT type of Christ's descent to the realm of death and his being heard there. Peter at Pentecost cites Ps 16:10 (“you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption”) as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection (Acts 2:27-31) — the same register of dying and being heard that Jonah's prayer inhabits. YHWH hears prayer from the depths of death: this is what the resurrection demonstrates.
2.3: A shadow: “All your waves and breakers swept over me.” The waves as the expression of YHWH's wrath against the guilty Jonah — the same language appears in Ps 88:7 (“your wrath lies heavy upon me, and you overwhelm me with all your waves”) and Ps 42:7 (“all your waves and your billows have gone over me”, cited in the passion context). Christ on the cross undergoes the full force of divine wrath — the waves of YHWH's judgment that Jonah experienced because of his own sin, Christ endured on behalf of ours (Isa 53:6: “the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all”).
2.4: A shadow: “I have been banished from your sight.” The experience of spiritual abandonment — separation from the presence of YHWH — is among the most devastating things in Jonah's prayer. Christ's cry from the cross — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matt 27:46, citing Ps 22:1) — is the ultimate, and most undeserved, form of this abandonment. Where Jonah is banished for his sin, Christ is forsaken for ours. The “banishment from YHWH's sight” that Jonah experiences as consequence, Christ endures as substitution: he is forsaken so that we need not be.
2.5: A shadow: “The engulfing waters threatened me, the deep surrounded me; seaweed was wrapped around my head.” The imagery of total immersion in the chaos-waters of death is the most visceral description of near-annihilation in Scripture. The passion narratives draw on the same Psalm tradition (Ps 69:1-2: “the waters have come up to my neck... I have come into deep waters”, cited in John 15:25 and Acts 1:20 in passion contexts). Christ's passion is the fullest possible immersion in human death and abandonment — not just symbolic drowning but actual death — so that those who trust him are pulled from the waters.
2.6: A type: “You brought my life up from the pit, O LORD my God.” The verb alah (brought up) from the shachat (pit, grave) is the resurrection language of the Psalms. Ps 16:10 (“you will not abandon my soul to the pit”) is cited by Peter (Acts 2:27) and Paul (Acts 13:35) as the OT text that anticipates Christ's resurrection. Jonah's emergence from the fish-belly is the enacted version of this psalm-prayer — being brought up from the pit. The type is explicit: the fish releases Jonah from death, as the tomb releases Christ on the third day, in both cases at YHWH's command.
2.7: A shadow: “When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, LORD, and my prayer rose to you.” In extremis — “when my life was ebbing away” — Jonah turns to YHWH. This is the pattern of faith in the abyss. Christ's final words from the cross follow this pattern: “Father, into your hands I commit my spirit” (Luke 23:46, citing Ps 31:5) is the ultimate “remembering YHWH” in the moment of death. Where Jonah's life ebbs because of his own flight, Christ's life ebbs by voluntary self-giving. Both prayers rise from the deepest place; both are heard; both result in life from death.
2.8: A shadow: “Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs.” This brief doctrinal statement within Jonah's prayer identifies the alternative to YHWH: idols that cannot respond, save, or offer mercy (chesed, steadfast love). Paul develops the same contrast in Romans: those who exchanged the truth of God for a lie (idols) are separated from the grace available in the one true God (Rom 1:21-25); but in Christ, grace is freely available to all who abandon idols and turn to the living God (1 Thess 1:9-10). Jonah preaches to idol-worshipping Nineveh; the gospel calls all idol-worshippers to the grace Jonah here confesses.
2.9: A direct anticipation: “Salvation is from the LORD” (yeshuah lADONAI). Jonah's confession from the fish's belly is the theological summary of the entire book — and the name of Jesus. The Hebrew yeshuah (salvation, deliverance) is the root of the name Yeshua (Jesus), which the angel explains as “he will save his people from their sins” (Matt 1:21). What Jonah confesses abstractly from within death — salvation belongs to YHWH alone, not to human effort or Gentile gods — Christ embodies concretely: he is the salvation that belongs to YHWH, made flesh and dwelling among us. The confession rises from the depths; the Savior descends into the depths to make the confession true.
2.10: A type: “The LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.” At YHWH's word, the fish releases its captive; Jonah emerges from death to new life and mission. This is the enacted version of the type Jesus names in Matt 12:40: just as Jonah came out of the fish on the third day, so the Son of Man will be raised from the heart of the earth. YHWH's authority over the fish — death cannot hold its captive beyond the appointed time — is the same authority that raises Christ: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it” (Acts 2:24). Death cannot hold the one YHWH releases.

3.1: A shadow: “The word of the LORD came to Jonah a second time.” The second commissioning — after failure, discipline, and rescue — is the OT pattern of divine recommissioning that reaches its fullest form in the resurrection appearances. The disciples who scattered at the arrest (Mark 14:50) and Peter who denied (John 18:17, 25-27) are recommissioned at the resurrection and at Pentecost: “Go and make disciples of all nations” (Matt 28:19). YHWH does not abandon his messengers after their failure; he restores and re-sends. The God of the second call is the God whose purposes cannot be finally thwarted by human disobedience.
3.2: A revelation of God: “Get up and go to Nineveh, that great city, and proclaim to it the message I give you.” The command is unchanged from the first calling (1:2); YHWH's purpose for Nineveh has not been altered by Jonah's flight. This reveals the perseverance of divine purpose — what YHWH intends to accomplish will not be prevented by human failures or delays. Christ's mission is characterized by this same perseverance: “I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it” (Matt 16:18). The gospel proclaimed to Nineveh cannot be stopped by a fleeing prophet; the gospel of Christ cannot be stopped by crucifixion or persecution.
3.3: A theme: “Jonah went to Nineveh, just as the LORD had commanded.” Obedience after discipline — full, unconditioned compliance with the divine command. The “just as the LORD had commanded” formula marks complete obedience. Christ's obedience is not disciplined obedience (corrected after failure) but perfect obedience from the start: “I always do what pleases him” (John 8:29); “obedient even to death, even death on a cross” (Phil 2:8). Jonah's finally-achieved obedience is a pale shadow of the obedience that characterizes the Son — but it demonstrates that the mission requires genuine human obedience, which only Christ perfectly embodies.
3.4: A shadow: Jonah's preaching to Nineveh — just five Hebrew words in the original (“Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown!”) — produces the greatest recorded revival in the OT. The minimal content of the message contrasts with the maximal effectiveness of the response. Christ says of John the Baptist: “Among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John” — and yet “something greater than Jonah is here” (Matt 12:41). If Jonah's spare announcement of judgment produced mass repentance, how much more the gospel of Christ's death and resurrection? The power is in the divine word, not in the messenger's eloquence or the message's length.
3.5: A shadow: “The people of Nineveh believed God.” Mass Gentile repentance in response to a Jewish prophet's preaching — the entire city, from greatest to least, responds to the word of YHWH. This anticipates the Gentile response to the gospel throughout Acts: Cornelius's household (Acts 10:44-48), the Greeks who “believed and turned to the Lord” (Acts 11:21), the “God-fearers” who respond more readily than the synagogue (Acts 13:48: “as many as were appointed to eternal life believed”). Jesus explicitly cites Nineveh's response as the standard against which his own generation is judged (Matt 12:41): Gentiles repented at the preaching of Jonah; this generation rejects something greater.
3.6: A shadow: the king of Nineveh descends from his throne, removes his robe, puts on sackcloth, and sits in ashes — a great king's voluntary humiliation before the word of YHWH. The voluntary descent from throne and honor is the structural shape of the incarnation: “though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant” (Phil 2:6-7). The Ninevite king's humiliation is compelled by fear of judgment; Christ's descent is motivated by love and accomplished through obedience. The shape is the same; the motive and the agent are incomparably different.
3.7: A theme: the royal decree of fasting and repentance — extending even to the animals — expresses the comprehensiveness of the response required. When YHWH's word reaches a city, it claims total allegiance, not selective compliance. The NT equivalent is the call to complete, wholehearted discipleship: “Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind” (Matt 22:37). Christ does not come to improve selective aspects of life but to claim the whole person — and the whole creation, which “waits with eager longing for the revealing of the sons of God” (Rom 8:19), is caught up in the redemption he accomplishes.
3.8: A revelation of God: “Who knows? God may turn and relent and turn from his fierce anger, so that we may not perish.” The Ninevite king's prayer expresses the heart of the OT's doctrine of repentance and divine responsiveness: YHWH is not mechanically determined to destroy; genuine repentance opens the possibility of mercy. This is the character YHWH himself announces in Exod 34:6-7 and Jonah confirms (4:2). Christ's death is the ground on which YHWH can relent not merely for Nineveh but for the whole world: the penalty falls on Christ, so that those who repent find mercy rather than judgment (Acts 3:19: “Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out”).
3.9: A revelation of God: “When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he relented and did not bring on them the disaster he had threatened.” Divine responsiveness to genuine human repentance — YHWH does what the king hoped he might (v9): he relents. This reveals the God who does not take pleasure in the death of the wicked (Ezek 18:23: “Have I any pleasure in the death of the wicked, declares the Lord GOD, and not rather that he should turn from his way and live?”). Christ's atonement makes this responsiveness permanently and perfectly available: those who repent find not a God who “may” relent (v9's “who knows?”) but one who has already acted in Christ to make full forgiveness possible.
3.10: A revelation of God: YHWH relents from the disaster he had announced — his mercy triumphs over judgment (Jas 2:13). The theological issue this creates (how can a God who announces judgment not follow through?) is the theodicy Jonah grapples with in ch. 4 and that Paul addresses in Romans: “Is God unjust? Not at all!” (Rom 9:14). The answer the NT provides is the cross: the judgment Nineveh escaped in ch. 3 does not simply disappear — it is borne by Christ (Rom 3:25-26: “God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... he did it to demonstrate his justice”). YHWH's relenting toward Nineveh is the shadow; Christ bearing the judgment so YHWH can justly relent toward all who repent is the substance.

4.1: A shadow: Jonah's anger at YHWH's mercy to Nineveh — “this greatly displeased Jonah, and he became angry” — is the OT form of the older brother's anger in the Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:28-30: “he became angry and refused to go in”). Both Jonah and the older brother are members of the covenant community who resent grace extended to the unworthy outsider. Christ embodies the opposite response: “there is more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance” (Luke 15:7). Jonah's anger about Nineveh's salvation is the anti-shape of Christ's joy over recovered sinners.
4.2: A revelation of God: Jonah quotes the Exodus 34:6-7 formula — “a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster” — as the reason for his anger; he resents precisely the character of YHWH that saves him. The irony cuts deep: the chesed (steadfast love) that extended to Nineveh is the same chesed that YHWH extends to Israel and to Jonah. In Christ, this character is expressed completely: “God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Rom 5:8). The grace Jonah resents for Nineveh is the grace that saves Jonah himself — and it reaches its fullest expression in the cross.
4.3: A theme: “Now, O LORD, please take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live.” Jonah's death-wish comes from the unbearable tension of being right about God's character but wrong about where that character should apply. The prophet who preached life to Nineveh wants death for himself. Christ, entering the fullness of human distress in Gethsemane (“My soul is very sorrowful, even to death”, Mark 14:34), does not flee from death but goes through it for others. The same three words — “it is better for me to die” — spoken in self-regarding bitterness by Jonah become in Christ an act of self-giving love: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death” (Phil 2:8).
4.4: A revelation of God: “Is it right for you to be angry?” YHWH's response to Jonah's death-wish is a question, not a condemnation. The divine pedagogy is patient: instead of rebuking the sulking prophet, YHWH asks a question that invites self-examination. Christ's pedagogy with his disciples follows this same pattern: patient questioning rather than crushing correction (Mark 8:29: “But who do you say that I am?”; John 21:15-17: “Simon, son of John, do you love me?”). The God who asks Jonah whether his anger is just is the God who in Christ asks Peter whether he loves him — both questions aim at the same inner transformation.
4.5: A theme: Jonah goes east of the city, builds a booth, and sits waiting to see what will happen — watching from a distance for the judgment that YHWH has already decided not to bring. The posture of the one who expects judgment rather than mercy. This is the posture of those who know God's power but struggle to receive his mercy as genuinely good news for others. Christ comes not to watch humanity from a distance but to enter into it completely: “the Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14). The booth Jonah builds for himself is the ironic shadow of the tent YHWH pitches in the incarnation.
4.6: A revelation of God: “The LORD God appointed a plant and made it come up over Jonah to give shade for his head, to save him from his discomfort.” Even in Jonah's anger and sulking, YHWH provides shade and comfort — YHWH cares for the prophet even when the prophet is behaving badly. This is the character Christ describes in Matt 5:45: “he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.” Divine provision is not contingent on human virtue. The plant-provision sets up the pedagogical argument of v10-11: if Jonah values what YHWH provided, how much more does YHWH value the 120,000 people of Nineveh?
4.7: A revelation of God: “At dawn the next day God appointed a worm, and it attacked the plant so that it withered.” The same God who provided the plant removes it — YHWH controls both provision and its withdrawal. This reveals the God of sovereign pedagogy: every gift is a lesson; every loss is teaching. The NT develops this as the “training” (paideia) of the Father: “the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives” (Heb 12:6, citing Prov 3:11-12). What happens to the plant in Jonah is the concrete enactment of what Hebrews 12 says about divine discipline — temporary removal of comfort for permanent transformation.
4.8: A theme: “The sun beat down on Jonah's head so that he grew faint.” Jonah in the scorching heat, fainting, asking again to die — the physical embodiment of spiritual despair. The prophet who proclaimed the word of God in Nineveh is now incapacitated by grief and heat. Christ enters this same extremity of physical suffering — “I thirst” (John 19:28, citing Ps 22:15: “my strength is dried up like a potsherd”) — but not in the paralysis of self-pity; in the consummation of self-giving. Both Jonah and Christ suffer; only one's suffering is redemptive.
4.9: A revelation of God: YHWH's second question — “Do you do well to be angry for the plant?” — and Jonah's defiant answer (“Yes, angry enough to die”) reveals the shape of misplaced value: Jonah is angrier about a plant than about the coming destruction of 120,000 people. The question that God asks is the question that exposes all misplaced priorities. Jesus's teaching ministry is structured around such questions: “What will it profit a man if he gains the whole world and forfeits his soul?” (Matt 16:26); “Is it lawful to do good or to do harm on the Sabbath, to save life or to destroy it?” (Mark 3:4). The questioner in Jonah 4 is the questioner in the Gospels.
4.10: A revelation of God: “You pity the plant, for which you did not labor, nor did you make it grow, which came into being in a night and perished in a night.” YHWH's argument from the lesser to the greater: if Jonah pities an uncreated, temporary plant he had no part in making, how much more does YHWH — who made the Ninevites and knows each one — pity them? The same argument structures the parables of Jesus: “If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him?” (Matt 7:11). The creator-creature argument reveals the character of the God who sent his only Son (John 3:16) to save what he made.
4.11: A direct anticipation: “Should I not pity Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left, and also much cattle?” The book ends here — with God's unanswered question. It is the OT's most direct statement of divine compassion for the ignorant, the helpless, and even the animals of a Gentile city. The NT's answer to this unanswered question is John 3:16: “For God so loved the world that he gave his only Son.” The “world” of John 3:16 is the Nineveh of Jonah 4:11 — vast, ignorant, undeserving, yet the object of divine pity. The Great Commission (Matt 28:19) is the action that the answer demands: go to all the Ninevehs of the world, because the God who pitied 120,000 Ninevites sent his Son for them all.
